name: Tantalum Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Tantalum. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Tantalum oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Tantalum's high reflectivity and thermal conductivity

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise oxide layer removal

repetitionRate.description: High repetition rate for uniform cleaning coverage on Tantalum surfaces

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for Tantalum oxide ablation threshold

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide ablation with minimal heat affected zone

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for efficient area coverage without thermal buildup

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete oxide removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal pulse overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat input

power.description: Typical range for industrial cleaning of reflective refractory metals like Tantalum to overcome high reflectivity (~95% at 1064 nm) while avoiding melting (mp 3017°C); starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for Nd:YAG systems; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Metals' (Optics & Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination to ensure complete removal; faster (up to 5000 mm/s) for light cleaning; 1000 mm/s recommended for optimal balance on Tantalum's conductive surface to minimize heat buildup. Source: Industry standards from Laser Institute of America guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-200 ns) preferred for industrial cleaning of Tantalum to ablate contaminants with minimal thermal diffusion due to its high thermal conductivity (57 W/m·K); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-low HAZ but costlier; 20 ns recommended. Source: Laser physics research in 'Pulsed Laser Cleaning of Refractory Metals' (Applied Surface Science, 2015).

repetition_rate.description: 10-200 kHz typical for cleaning; lower rates reduce heat accumulation on heat-resistant Tantalum, higher speeds up process but risk overheating; 50 kHz recommended for efficient contamination removal without substrate damage. Trade-off: high freq increases throughput but requires cooling. Source: Academic literature in Journal of Laser Applications (2019).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for cleaning contaminants from Tantalum; damage threshold ~5-10 J/cm² due to high reflectivity and melting point; 2 J/cm² recommended for safe ablation below threshold. Conservative for uncertain specific data on Tantalum. Source: Ablation threshold studies in 'Laser Interaction with Refractory Metals' (Journal of Applied Physics, 2017).

spot_size.description: 100-500 μm for fine detail cleaning on Tantalum components (e.g., implants); up to 1000 μm for large-area surface prep; 300 μm recommended for uniform energy distribution on reflective surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and SPI Lasers for 1064 nm systems.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination; 2-5 passes for heavy oxide or residue removal on Tantalum; 2 passes recommended to achieve complete cleaning without excessive heat exposure. Source: Industry case studies in aerospace applications (e.g., NASA reports on refractory metal processing, 2020).

overlap_ratio.description: 30-80% overlap ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for Tantalum to prevent gaps in cleaning while minimizing over-processing and heat accumulation on conductive surfaces. Source: Process optimization studies in 'Optimization of Laser Cleaning Parameters' (Surface and Coatings Technology, 2016).
